title: IAM Roles, Least Privilege, and Explicit Deny
description:
## Giving a VM-hosted app access to object storage

An application running on a cloud VM needs read/write access to one object-storage bucket. A developer proposes baking a long-lived access key + secret into the app's config file. A reviewer also asks how policy evaluation resolves when one policy allows an action and another explicitly denies it. Which approach and explanation are correct?

options.text: Attach an IAM role to the VM (an instance profile) so the app obtains short-lived, automatically-rotated credentials via STS instead of embedding static keys; scope the role's policy to only the required actions on that one bucket (least privilege). In IAM evaluation an explicit Deny always overrides any Allow, so a guardrail Deny on, say, deleting other buckets wins even if a broad Allow is also attached.

Embedding a long-lived access key is fine as long as it is encrypted at rest; roles are only for cross-account access. When an Allow and an explicit Deny both apply, the most recently attached policy wins.

Attach an IAM role, but note that roles grant *permanent* credentials just like access keys; the only benefit is avoiding a config file. An explicit Deny is overridden by an explicit Allow that is more specific (e.g. names the exact resource ARN).

Use the cloud account's root credentials on the VM so the app never hits permission errors, then restrict it later; in policy evaluation, Allow and Deny cancel out, leaving the action implicitly denied.

explanation: The correct pattern is to attach an **IAM role** to the instance (an instance profile). The app then retrieves **temporary, automatically-rotated** credentials issued by STS from the instance metadata service — there is no long-lived secret to leak, commit, or rotate manually. The role's policy should grant **only** the specific actions on the specific bucket (least privilege). This refutes (b) ("embedding a key is fine") and (c) ("roles grant permanent credentials") — role credentials are explicitly *short-lived*, which is the whole point. On evaluation order: across all applicable identity and resource policies, an **explicit Deny always wins** over any Allow, regardless of specificity or attachment order; the absence of an Allow is an *implicit* deny, and any *explicit* Deny is final. That makes (b)'s "most recent wins," (c)'s "specific Allow overrides Deny," and (d)'s "Allow and Deny cancel out" all wrong. (d) is also a serious anti-pattern: using root credentials on a workload violates least privilege and never gets safely "restricted later."

hints.title: Roles over keys
hints.body: Attaching a role to compute yields temporary credentials via STS that rotate automatically — no static secret in code or config to be stolen.

hints.title: Deny is absolute
hints.body: IAM is default-deny; an Allow grants, but any explicit Deny that matches the request overrides every Allow, independent of order or specificity.
